body_txt: Just as the story of Lucifer is about betrayal , so can we expect the devil to be betrayed in turn . The first component of this trope is that a Deal with the Devil must be made, an agreement between the protagonist and an evil figure. note Even the Trope Namer , Faust , substitutes the archdemon Mephistopheles for the capital 'D' devil. Jackass Genie interpretation of the contract and Read the Fine Print is optional. The second component of this trope is that the protagonist gains some sort of power from this agreement. Usually, these powers are part of the villain's evil theme . The third part is rebelling against the evil figure. This may be a Heel–Face Turn but is much more likely to come from someone who was Good All Along and doesn't want to be a pawn in the devil's schemes. If the original contract has clauses against rebelling, expect a Zeroth Law Rebellion . Sub-Trope to Create Your Own Hero , Deal with the Devil , and Bad Powers, Good People . Can cause a Plot Hole if it's not explained why the evil figure can't just take his powers back, or a Voodoo Shark if the explanation raises more questions than it answers. See also Hoist by His Own Petard , Did You Just Scam Cthulhu? , Phlebotinum Rebel , and Dark Is Not Evil . A subversion of Evil Is Not a Toy . Compare Pro-Human Transhuman , Turned Against Their Masters and Sheep in Wolf's Clothing . Contrast The Punishment (where the powers were gained as a result of torture/curse). Is often done by the Anti-Anti-Christ .
